ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for ayam geprek. FOODON_00002764 (fried chicken dish) is the closest defensible anchor. The rice and sambal components are not captured by this single term.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken meat, fried — used as the primary protein component anchor for this mixed rice dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a composite code for ayam geprek as a complete dish. A0CKP (fried chicken) is used as the primary component anchor. The steamed rice and sambal bawang components are not individually coded here.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for ayam geprek or equivalent Indonesian fried chicken rice dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 400 g, comprising a portion of deep-fried smashed chicken (roughly 150–180 g) served over steamed white rice (approximately 180–200 g) with a spicy onion sambal relish (approximately 30–40 g).

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat and skin, cooked, fried, batter (FDC ID 171477) and Rice, white, long-grain, regular, cooked (FDC ID 168878), combined with Singapore HPB Foodo sambal reference for condiment component
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Ayam geprek as a complete dish is not listed in any single approved source. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component averaging: approximately 40% deep-fried battered chicken (USDA FDC 171477), approximately 48% steamed white rice (USDA FDC 168878), and approximately 12% sambal bawang (chilli-shallot-garlic relish, estimated from HPB Singapore condiment references). Input per_100g values from the source record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are broadly consistent with this component-weighted estimate and have been retained with medium confidence. Sodium value of 300 mg/100g is plausible given sambal contribution.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — White rice (steamed, long-grain) GI ~72 used as primary carbohydrate driver for this mixed rice dish.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value exists for ayam geprek as a complete dish in the University of Sydney GI Database. The GI of 72 is assigned based on steamed white rice (long-grain) as the dominant carbohydrate source, consistent with published GI values for white rice in the University of Sydney GI Database and the 2008 International Tables (Atkinson et al.). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 45.0) / 100 = 32.4, where carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 11.25 × 4 = 45.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content of 300 mg per 100 g, yielding approximately 1200 mg sodium per standard 400 g serving — equivalent to 52% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2300 mg in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to high sodium from sambal and fried chicken seasoning; if consumed, request reduced salt in sambal preparation and limit portion to no more than 200 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 72) driven by steamed white rice as the dominant carbohydrate source, with low dietary fibre (0.75 g/100 g) providing minimal glucose-buffering effect, resulting in a high glycemic load of 32.4 per standard serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice to reduce glycemic load, limit portion size to no more than 200 g, and add non-starchy vegetables to slow postprandial glucose absorption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying contributes saturated fat (3.0 g/100 g) and trans fat (0.13 g/100 g), alongside high sodium (300 mg/100 g) and cholesterol (37.5 mg/100 g), all of which are established cardiovascular risk factors.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid deep-fried preparations; choose grilled or baked chicken instead, limit sambal portion to reduce sodium intake, and monitor total saturated fat consumption within the context of daily dietary targets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote systemic inflammation; chilli in sambal is a nightshade ingredient that may exacerbate joint inflammation in RA-sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried preparations due to pro-inflammatory AGE formation and consider omitting or reducing the chilli-based sambal to minimise nightshade-related joint flare risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ayam Geprek Spicy Level 2 with Spicy Onion Relish is an Indonesian-origin dish widely served in Singapore's food delivery and hawker-adjacent restaurant scene. The dish consists of a deep-fried whole chicken piece — typically a thigh or drumstick — that is battered, fried until golden and crispy, then smashed (geprek) and pressed into a freshly pounded sambal bawang (spicy onion relish) made from red chillies, shallots, and garlic, often mixed with hot cooking oil to bloom the aromatics. The chicken is served over a mound of steamed white rice. The spicy level designation (Level 2) indicates a moderate-to-assertive heat level, with the sambal providing both pungency from chilli and a sweet-savoury depth from caramelised shallots and garlic. Texturally, the dish contrasts crispy fried chicken skin against soft steamed rice and the oily, fragrant sambal. Nutritionally, a standard 400 g serving provides approximately 750 kcal, 45 g protein, 45 g fat, and 45 g carbohydrate. The dish is high in sodium (~1200 mg per serving) and has a high glycemic index (GI 72) driven by white rice, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. It is halal-certified in most commercial preparations and gluten-free by default. The dish is culturally rooted in Indonesian street food tradition and has become a popular fast-casual option across Southeast Asia, including Singapore where it is commonly available via food delivery platforms.

confidence_reason: Ayam geprek as a complete dish is not listed in any single approved primary nutrient database. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted component average of USDA FoodData Central entries for deep-fried battered chicken and steamed white rice, supplemented by HPB Singapore condiment references for the sambal component. The input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are broadly consistent with this estimate. Medium confidence is assigned because no exact dish-level match exists in an approved source.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, full micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite Indonesian fried chicken and rice dish are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central, Singapore HPB Foodo, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases as of 2024. trans_fat_g of 0.13 g/100g is retained from the input record as a plausible trace value consistent with deep-frying in vegetable oil; this is not confirmed zero. All core macros (protein_g 11.25, fat_g 11.25, carbohydrate_g 11.25) are non-zero and reflect the mixed composition of fried chicken, steamed rice, and sambal. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on steamed white rice as the dominant carbohydrate driver per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. Glycemic load per 400 g serving = 32.4. The dish is Indonesian in origin but is sold in Singapore; multilingual naming has been applied for all Singaporean-market mandatory languages (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as well as Indonesian. Chinese names are descriptive renderings (香辣葱蒜炸鸡饭（二级辣）/ 香辣蔥蒜炸雞飯（二級辣）) as no standard Chinese name exists for this dish. Malay, Tamil, and Hindi names are transliterations or descriptive equivalents appropriate for the Singapore market context. Aliases self-check: all strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in the flat aliases array — verified.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft produced with Indonesian-only multilingual naming.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'.

Iteration 3: Added mandatory Singaporean-market local-language names to local_names — chinese_simplified ('香辣葱蒜炸鸡饭（二级辣）'), chinese_traditional ('香辣蔥蒜炸雞飯（二級辣）'), malay ('Ayam Geprek Sambal Bawang Pedas Tahap 2'), tamil ('அயம் கெப்ரெக் காரமான வெங்காய சட்னி (நிலை 2)'), hindi ('आयम गेप्रेक मसालेदार प्याज चटनी (स्तर 2)') — to satisfy G6 validator requirement for dishes sold in Singapore. Added all new local-language strings to aliases_by_language and flat aliases array. Verified aliases consistency self-check.
